preamble: The voiced dental fricative /ð/ can undergo a phonetic transformation to the voiced palatal approximant /j/. This shift is a form of lenition, where a fricative weakens to an approximant, and often involves palatalization, where the point of articulation moves from the dental region to the palatal region. This change is typically conditioned by specific phonetic environments, such as occurring intervocalically or in palatalized contexts.
phoneticEffects: Lenition (fricative to approximant), Palatalization (dental to palatal)

examples.note: Intervocalic /ð/ shifting to /j/, which can then be lost.
Unattested: Phonetic shift of /ð/ to /j/, sometimes represented by orthographic <G> or <I>.
Shift of /ð/ to /j/ or its loss before /i/.

sources: ROP TÚ MO BAILE: GRADIENT, RECURRENT APOCOPE AND SYNCOPE IN THE DEVELOPMENT OF IRISH.
Golinets, V. (2018). AMORITE NAMES THROUGH TIME AND SPACE. Oxford University Press.
Rohleder, J. (2024). A grammar of Vamale. Language Science Press.
Karatsareas, S. (2019). Agglutinative Noun Inflection in Cappadocian. SciSpace.
